Title: Biosynthetic Pathway and Metabolic Engineering of Plant Dihydrochalcones
Journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A primary engineering target for increasing dihydrochalcone flux; should be co-expressed with CHS and downstream glycosyltransferases in heterologous hosts or edited/overexpressed in apple to raise phloretin-derived metabolites. Because it is the first committed branch enzyme from general phenylpropanoid metabolism, deployment should consider flux competition with other phenylpropanoids.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the alpha,beta-unsaturated double bond of hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA esters such as p-coumaroyl-CoA and feruloyl-CoA to form the corresponding dihydro-CoA esters, including p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA and dihydroferuloyl-CoA, using NADPH.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA, the entry substrate for phloretin and other dihydrochalcones, thereby enabling dihydrochalcone accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation; recombinant enzyme assay; kinetic analysis; identification of in vivo products in apple leaves.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate committed precursor of phloretin and downstream dihydrochalcones.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Reported/validated in apple leaves where in vivo products were detected; detailed tissue profile not given in this review.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review identifies this enzyme as the first committed enzyme in dihydrochalcone biosynthesis and notes that ENRL3/5 or other reductases may also contribute in planta.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Entry step from general phenylpropanoid pathway into the dihydrochalcone branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert p-coumaroyl-CoA and feruloyl-CoA away from other phenylpropanoid products.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as first committed branch-point enzyme and key control step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MdHCDBR catalyzes NADPH-dependent reduction of hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA esters to dihydro-CoA esters. 2. Its in vivo products in apple leaves include p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA and dihydroferuloyl-CoA. 3. The enzyme defines the first committed branch from general phenylpropanoid metabolism into dihydrochalcone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Apple leaves, peel, flesh, seeds as discussed for apple dihydrochalcones
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant growth and stress response; potential human health benefits including anti-cancer and other biomedical activities

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering of phloretin production in plants and microbes; likely should be stacked with DBR and glycosyltransferases to improve final dihydrochalcone yield. Choice among CHS isoforms may matter because substrate conversion rates differ.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses one p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA starter with three malonyl-CoA units to form phloretin; can also accept p-coumaroyl-CoA to form naringenin chalcone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables conversion of committed dihydro-CoA precursor into the dihydrochalcone backbone phloretin, thereby promoting downstream dihydrochalcone formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional characterization of recombinant proteins and kinetic assays with p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA and p-coumaroyl-CoA.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the dihydrochalcone backbone precursor phloretin.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Three apple CHS genes were functionally characterized; the review notes canonical CHS may catalyze phloretin formation in some plant families, but pathway specificity across plants remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because CHS also uses p-coumaroyl-CoA for flavonoid biosynthesis, engineering may alter flux between flavonoids and dihydrochalcones.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Phloretin from p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA; naringenin chalcone from p-coumaroyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important core scaffold-forming step; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Apple CHS enzymes accept both canonical and dihydro starter substrates. 2. They can produce phloretin from p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA. 3. This supports CHS participation in dihydrochalcone backbone formation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant stress-related metabolites with potential health-promoting bioactivities

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering of phloretin production in plants and microbes; likely should be stacked with DBR and glycosyltransferases to improve final dihydrochalcone yield. Choice among CHS isoforms may matter because substrate conversion rates differ.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses one p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA starter with three malonyl-CoA units to form phloretin; can also accept p-coumaroyl-CoA to form naringenin chalcone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables conversion of committed dihydro-CoA precursor into the dihydrochalcone backbone phloretin, thereby promoting downstream dihydrochalcone formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional characterization of recombinant proteins and kinetic assays with p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA and p-coumaroyl-CoA.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the dihydrochalcone backbone precursor phloretin.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Three apple CHS genes were functionally characterized; the review notes canonical CHS may catalyze phloretin formation in some plant families, but pathway specificity across plants remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because CHS also uses p-coumaroyl-CoA for flavonoid biosynthesis, engineering may alter flux between flavonoids and dihydrochalcones.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Phloretin from p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA; naringenin chalcone from p-coumaroyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important core scaffold-forming step; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Apple CHS enzymes accept both canonical and dihydro starter substrates. 2. They can produce phloretin from p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA. 3. This supports CHS participation in dihydrochalcone backbone formation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant stress-related metabolites with potential health-promoting bioactivities

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A particularly well-characterized apple CHS isoform for engineering phloretin biosynthesis; should be paired with DBR and downstream glycosyltransferases for dihydrochalcone production in microbial or plant systems.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA with three malonyl-CoA molecules to form phloretin; also accepts p-coumaroyl-CoA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly supports phloretin formation, the core backbone of major apple dihydrochalcones.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein assays and kinetic characterization; reported Km values for p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA and p-coumaroyl-CoA.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms phloretin, the immediate core product of the pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Table 1 reports kinetic values toward p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA (Km 5.07 uM) and p-coumaroyl-CoA (Km 5.09 uM).
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can also channel flux to flavonoid precursors depending on substrate availability.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key scaffold-forming step, not explicitly shown as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MdCHS3 efficiently accepts p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA. 2. It forms the phloretin scaffold needed for downstream glycosylated dihydrochalcones. 3. Its biochemical properties support a direct role in apple dihydrochalcone biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant growth/stress-related specialized metabolite with potential human health benefits

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strong candidate for engineering phloridzin accumulation in apple or heterologous systems; can be stacked downstream of DBR and CHS for direct conversion of phloretin to a stable storage glycoside.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to the 2'-hydroxyl of phloretin to form phloridzin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of phloridzin, the major glycosylated dihydrochalcone in apple.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation; recombinant protein assay; substrate specificity assay; kinetic characterization.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the major terminal/storage product phloridzin from phloretin.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Described as accepting only phloretin as substrate in one characterization; likely corresponds to apple phloretin-specific glycosyltransferase activity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could reduce free phloretin pool while increasing stable glycoside storage.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important terminal determinant for phloridzin accumulation; not formally established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MdPGT1 glycosylates phloretin at the 2'-hydroxyl. 2. The product is phloridzin, a major apple dihydrochalcone. 3. This reaction likely stabilizes reactive phloretin into an accumulated storage form.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite implicated in plant responses and valued for potential human health effects

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful downstream engineering enzyme for phloridzin production; broad substrate acceptance may permit flexible metabolic engineering but may also cause side products.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose to the 2'-hydroxyl of phloretin in vivo, although recombinant enzyme shows activity toward several flavonoids and phenylpropanoids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely contributes to phloridzin biosynthesis from phloretin in apple.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein assay and substrate testing; inferred in vivo function from enzyme activity and apple metabolite context.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms phloridzin or contributes to its terminal glycosylation step.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Broad substrate acceptance in vitro; true in vivo substrate range may be narrower.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad acceptor specificity may create off-target glycosides in engineered systems.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT71A15 accepts phloretin in vitro. 2. Its likely in vivo role is phloretin 2'-O-glucosylation. 3. It may contribute to phloridzin accumulation in apple.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant specialized metabolite with putative defense/stress roles and human health relevance

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful downstream engineering enzyme for phloridzin production; broad substrate acceptance may permit flexible metabolic engineering but may also cause side products.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose to the 2'-hydroxyl of phloretin in vivo, although recombinant enzyme shows activity toward several flavonoids and phenylpropanoids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely contributes to phloridzin biosynthesis from phloretin in apple.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein assay and substrate testing; inferred in vivo function from enzyme activity and apple metabolite context.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms phloridzin or contributes to its terminal glycosylation step.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Broad substrate acceptance in vitro; true in vivo substrate range may be narrower.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad acceptor specificity may create off-target glycosides in engineered systems.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT71K1 accepts phloretin in vitro. 2. Its likely in vivo role is phloretin 2'-O-glucosylation. 3. It may contribute to phloridzin accumulation in apple.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant specialized metabolite with putative defense/stress roles and human health relevance

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A position-specific enzyme valuable for engineering trilobatin production from phloretin; useful for tailoring the glycoside spectrum of apple dihydrochalcones rather than only increasing phloridzin.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to the 4'-hydroxyl of phloretin to form trilobatin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of trilobatin from phloretin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation; recombinant enzyme assay; substrate specificity analysis; kinetic characterization; in vivo product identification.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms a terminal glycosylated dihydrochalcone product.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highly position specific for phloretin, though it also accepts trilobatin, phloridzin, quercetin, and naringenin at lower efficiency.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May partition phloretin away from phloridzin synthesis, changing the dihydrochalcone profile.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant for trilobatin branch; not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MdPh-4'-OGT specifically glucosylates phloretin at the 4'-hydroxyl. 2. Its in vivo product is trilobatin. 3. The enzyme provides a route to diversify the apple dihydrochalcone glycoside profile.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite with potential health-related bioactivity and flavor relevance

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to engineer stable C-glycosyl dihydrochalcones such as nothofagin in heterologous systems because C-glycosides are chemically stable.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes C-glucosylation at the 3'-position of an appropriate dihydrochalcone-related acceptor to form nothofagin; review states OsCGT forms the 3'-C-glycoside nothofagin exclusively.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly produces nothofagin, a C-glycosylated dihydrochalcone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation and recombinant protein enzymatic assay.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms a terminal C-glycosylated dihydrochalcone product.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes C-glycosides are unusually stable because the sugar is attached through a C-C bond.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert substrate into highly stable C-glycosides that are harder to remobilize.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsCGT forms the 3'-C-glycoside nothofagin. 2. It demonstrates a route to stable C-glycosyl dihydrochalcones. 3. The gene is useful for heterologous production of nothofagin.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite; C-glycosides are chemically stable and of interest for bioactivity and engineering

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering C-glycosylated dihydrochalcones in microbes or plants; can expand the diversity of stable DHC derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes C-glucosylation of phloretin to produce C-glycosylated dihydrochalcones such as nothofagin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation and recombinant protein assay.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms terminal C-glycosylated DHC product.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review identifies FeCGTa as UGT708C1.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could alter the DHC composition toward very stable C-glycosides.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FeCGTa/UGT708C1 C-glucosylates phloretin. 2. It supports biosynthesis of stable C-glycosyl dihydrochalcones. 3. The enzyme broadens the known DHC modification toolkit.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite with potential bioactivity and enhanced chemical stability

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering C-glycosylated dihydrochalcones in microbes or plants; can expand the diversity of stable DHC derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes C-glucosylation of phloretin to produce C-glycosylated dihydrochalcones such as nothofagin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation and recombinant protein assay.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms terminal C-glycosylated DHC product.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review identifies FeCGTb as UGT708C2.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could alter the DHC composition toward very stable C-glycosides.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FeCGTb/UGT708C2 C-glucosylates phloretin. 2. It supports biosynthesis of stable C-glycosyl dihydrochalcones. 3. The enzyme broadens the known DHC modification toolkit.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite with potential bioactivity and enhanced chemical stability

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering of di-C-glucosyl phloretin derivatives in plant or microbial platforms.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of di-C-glucosylated phloretin derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation and reported enzymatic function in review.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Reported together with FuCGT as catalyzing di-C-glucosyl phloretin formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert phloretin to highly modified end products.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CuCGT catalyzes di-C-glucosyl phloretin formation. 2. It expands known DHC glycosylation chemistry beyond mono-C-glycosides. 3. It is a candidate tool for diversifying DHC products in engineering systems.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite with potential enhanced stability and bioactivity

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering of di-C-glucosyl phloretin derivatives in plant or microbial platforms.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of di-C-glucosylated phloretin derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA isolation and reported enzymatic function in review.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Reported together with CuCGT as catalyzing di-C-glucosyl phloretin formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert phloretin to highly modified end products.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FuCGT catalyzes di-C-glucosyl phloretin formation. 2. It expands known DHC glycosylation chemistry beyond mono-C-glycosides. 3. It is a candidate tool for diversifying DHC products in engineering systems.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite with potential enhanced stability and bioactivity

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Transgenic apple lines overexpressing heterologous gene; cultivar not specified here
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful to engineer 3-hydroxylated dihydrochalcones in apple and other systems, but because 3-hydroxyphloretin is reactive and is immediately glycosylated, co-engineering with suitable glycosyltransferases is advisable.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the 3-position of dihydrochalcones/chalcones, enabling conversion toward 3-hydroxyphloridzin-related products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in apple and metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms a modified derivative of the phloretin/phloridzin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Assessed in transgenic apple overexpression context
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CPR (implied requirement for cytochrome P450 activity)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In transgenic apple overexpressing Cosmos sulphureus CH3H, no 3-hydroxyphloretin accumulated, implying rapid conversion to 3-hydroxyphloridzin to avoid toxicity/cell damage.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reactive 3-hydroxyphloretin may cause cell damage if not rapidly glycosylated; engineering should include detoxifying/storage steps.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Microsome / endoplasmic reticulum-associated (inferred for cytochrome P450)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CH3H introduces a 3-hydroxyl group into the DHC branch. 2. Overexpression in apple increases 3-hydroxyphloridzin. 3. The free 3-hydroxyphloretin intermediate appears too reactive to accumulate and is rapidly glycosylated.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolite likely contributing to defense/stress functions and potentially altered bioactivity

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: RNAi silencing construct promoter not specified in review
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A candidate positive regulator/enzyme contributor for boosting DHC accumulation, but because its exact contribution versus HCDBR remains unresolved, it should be used cautiously and validated alongside HCDBR. Silencing suggests it supports pathway flux.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduction of ENRL-3 transcripts caused about a 66% decrease in leaf dihydrochalcone concentration in one silenced line, indicating a positive role in DHC biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi-based transcript downregulation in transgenic apple and metabolite measurement.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Conditionally important contributor to DHC production in apple leaves; evidence suggests it substantially supports pathway flux but may not be the only reductase involved.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Assessed in apple leaves in RNAi transgenic lines
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Precursor supply to dihydrochalcone biosynthesis via reduction of p-coumaroyl-CoA to p-dihydrocoumaroyl-CoA
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified here as regulatory/supporting due to unresolved exact enzymatic identity in the review; the review notes ENRL-3/5 and other reductases may share or overlap this step.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential alteration of broader phenylpropanoid flux if manipulated.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive control of pathway flux inferred from transcript reduction phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Not a gene-expression regulator; positively affects DHC biosynthetic output
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ENRL-3 silencing in Royal Gala reduced leaf DHC content by about 66% in one line. 2. This supports a role in generating the reduced precursor for DHC biosynthesis. 3. The review notes that ENRL proteins may act alongside HCDBR or other reductases.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant stress- and growth-related specialized metabolites with potential human health benefits

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: RNAi silencing construct promoter not specified in review
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A valuable gene target for tuning the broader apple polyphenol profile and dihydrochalcone levels, but manipulation may cause wide metabolic ripple effects because downregulation changes many polyphenolic compounds.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Targeted downregulation changes concentrations of a wide range of polyphenolic compounds, including the dihydrochalcone phloretin.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted downregulation in apple and metabolite profiling.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as a major determinant of phloretin glycosylation status and influences the broader polyphenol metabolite profile.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Dihydrochalcone glycosylation and broader polyphenol remodeling
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although it is an enzyme, the review emphasizes its pathway-wide phenotypic effect upon downregulation; exact regiospecific product is not detailed in the provided text.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad changes to many polyphenolic compounds indicate pleiotropic metabolic effects.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Phloretin and related polyphenol glycosylation outputs
Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Metabolic modifier / glycosyltransferase with pathway-wide impact
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Not a transcriptional regulator; alters metabolite profile by changing glycosylation reactions
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Downregulation of UGT88F1 alters many apple polyphenols. 2. Phloretin among the dihydrochalcones is significantly affected. 3. The gene is therefore important for shaping DHC and broader phenolic metabolite composition.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Specialized metabolites involved in plant physiology/stress response and of human health interest
